title: Deep Memory of iOS
description: In-depth analysis of iOS memory: From Memory Graph to OOM (Out of Memory) location. Detailed explanation of Dirty/Clean Memory, Compressed Memory mechanisms, and memory optimization strategies.
content:
## Memory Footprint

```mermaid
graph TD
  subgraph Typical_app_memory_profile
    Dirty_memory
    Clean_memory
    Compressed
  end

```
  
```mermaid
graph LR

  subgraph Memory_warnings
    App_is_not_always_the_cause
    Compressor_complicates_freeing_memory
    Prefer_policy_changes_over_cache_purging
  end

  Memory_warnings -.-> Pages

  subgraph Pages
    Page_types --> Dirty_memory
    Page_types --> Clean_memory
    Typically_16KB
  end
  
  subgraph Memory_mapped_files
    File-backed_memory
    Read-only_files_are_clean
    Kernel_manages_when_file_is_in_RAM
  end

  Memory_mapped_files --> files

  subgraph files
    image.jpeg
    Blob.data
    Training.model
  end
  

  Clean_memory --> Data_that_can_be_paged_out_of_memory
  Data_that_can_be_paged_out_of_memory --> Memory_mapped_files
  Data_that_can_be_paged_out_of_memory --> __DATA_CONST

  subgraph Frameworks
  __DATA_CONST
  __DATA
  __DATA_DIRTY
  end

  Dirty_memory --> Memory_written_by_an_app
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> All_heap_allocations
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> Decoded_image_buffers
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> __DATA
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> __DATA_DIRTY

  All_heap_allocations --> malloc
  All_heap_allocations --> Allay
  All_heap_allocations --> NSCache
  All_heap_allocations --> UIViews
  All_heap_allocations --> String

  Decoded_image_buffers --> CGRasterData
  Decoded_image_buffers --> ImageIO
  

  subgraph Memory_compressor
    No_traditional_disk_swap
    Compresses_unaccessed_pages
    Decompresses_pages_upon_access
  end

  Memory_compressor --> Pages

```


### Pages
It consists of multiple pages, each occupying 16KB. If that's not enough, more will be requested.

`Memory is use` = `Number of Pages` X `Page size`
### Clean & dirty pages

Clean Memory
:Memory-mapped files: These are files loaded from disk into memory but not written to or modified by the application, such as images, data blocks, and training models.

Read-only data: This includes constant data or read-only global variables in the application, such as strings and constant arrays.

In short, data that can be paged out of memory can be paginated out of memory by the operating system to reclaim memory space. Paged data is typically stored in other areas of the disk, such as page files or swap partitions (swap partitions do not exist in iOS), so it can be reloaded into memory when needed.

Dirty Memory

:Dynamically allocated objects: Objects dynamically created using methods like malloc and calloc, such as dynamically allocated strings and arrays.

Object property modification: Modifying or updating existing objects, including assigning values ​​to properties and modifying array elements.

Image buffer decoding: Decoding images, converting compressed formats back to raw pixel data in memory.

Cached data: Caching mechanisms implemented by the application itself, used to store frequently accessed data, such as network request results and calculation results.

In short, memory written by an app primarily comes from data created by the app.### Frameworks
A good practice is to use the singleton pattern and global initializers of frameworks to alleviate the amount of dirty memory generated by their usage.

Using the singleton pattern and global initializers in frameworks is a good practice to reduce the amount of dirty memory generated during their use.

The singleton pattern is a design pattern that ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a global access point. When objects in a framework are created using the singleton pattern, they persist in memory, reducing frequent memory allocation and deallocation operations, thus reducing the generation of dirty memory.

Global initializers are initializers that run when the framework is linked or the class is loaded. By performing necessary operations and initialization steps in the global initializer, dirty memory generated during the framework's use can be reduced.

In short, achieving this effect is similar to defining a static variable as const.(const is determined at compile time.)An immutable design paradigm.

If only a singleton is designed, but its properties and methods are dynamically created and modified, they will ultimately end up in dirty memory.

### Compressed
This capability was introduced in iOS 7.

In an operating system, memory is a finite resource. To maximize the use of available memory, memory compression technology was introduced. The memory compressor monitors the system's memory usage and compresses pages in memory as needed. Unused pages typically occupy more memory; compressing these unused pages reduces memory usage.

When an application needs to access a compressed page, the memory compressor decompresses the page before accessing it. This process is transparent; the application is unaware that a page has been compressed. By decompressing on access, the memory compressor ensures that the application can access the required data correctly.

Memory compression technology is particularly effective for systems or applications with a large number of unused pages. It can reduce memory usage, extend the lifespan of available memory, and improve overall system performance. However, it's important to note that memory compression and decompression processes increase CPU load and may cause slight access delays.

In short, compressed memory may suddenly increase during use, potentially causing CPU spikes.### Memory warnings
What should you do when your app is experiencing memory shortages? And why does this memory shortage sometimes occur?

```mermaid
graph TD
  subgraph Typical_app_memory_profile
    Dirty_memory
    Clean_memory
    Compressed

    subgraph id[Memory_warnings]
      App_is_not_always_the_cause
      Compressor_complicates_freeing_memory
      Prefer_policy_changes_over_cache_purging
    end
    style id fill:#bbf,stroke:#f66,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,stroke-dasharray: 5 5


  end
```
